items.q: Why is the global energy transition slower than many expect?
items.correct: Existing fossil-fuel infrastructure is sunk cost, low-carbon alternatives need vast capital, intermittency requires storage, and political/economic interests resist change — even as costs of renewables fall.
items.misconception: The energy transition is slow because renewable energy doesn't actually work.
items.random_wrong: The transition is slow because people don't want to give up cars.
items.why: Renewables are now often cheaper than fossil fuels per unit. The barriers are infrastructure, finance, grid integration and politics — a system inertia problem, not a technology limitation.

items.q: Why might managed retreat be more sustainable than building a sea wall?
items.correct: Sea walls are expensive, deflect erosion elsewhere and reflect wave energy worsening beach loss; managed retreat works with natural processes, creates habitat and is cheaper long-term.
items.misconception: Sea walls always solve coastal erosion problems permanently.
items.random_wrong: Managed retreat means letting all coastal towns disappear into the sea.
items.why: Sea walls protect one stretch but cause terminal scour and sediment starvation downdrift. Managed retreat selectively allows the coast to adjust — controversial socially but ecologically sound.

items.q: What is the difference between outsourcing and offshoring?
items.correct: Outsourcing means contracting work to another company; offshoring means relocating work to another country. A firm can outsource without offshoring, or offshore without outsourcing (in-house overseas).
items.misconception: Outsourcing and offshoring are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Outsourcing means moving to another country and offshoring means hiring contractors.
items.why: The distinction matters for analysis: outsourcing is about who does the work (firm boundary); offshoring is about where (geography). Many real cases are both — e.g. UK firms outsourcing call centres to Indian contractors.
Correct answer: Relph. “Tuan” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Ruth Glass. “Doreen Massey” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Liverpool European Capital of Culture?
items.why: Exact answer: 2008 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
